quotes.verbatim_quote:
Do not place fingers in position in advance - left and right hand attack must be together for best results

in articulated situations - velocity demands it
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets the core articulation rule that left-hand placement and right-hand attack must be simultaneous, which underwrites every articulated passage in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The following study is the same pattern as the others in this chapter with this difference:

The overall spread of notes is one octave instead of a tenth
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the chapter's compressed sixths-with-upper-line variant by contrasting its one-octave spread against the tenth-spread default that governs the surrounding studies.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The whole and half notes must be sustained full value; keep the pressure applied firmly while the quarter notes are in

motion, The left hand must be trained to apply and hold pressure with any one of the fingers while the others articulate with relaxed control,

power, and agility.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies the lap-piano texture demand: sustained outer voices held under full pressure while remaining fingers articulate the inner line independently.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
THE SHAPES OF FINGERBOARD SOUNDS—THE EARS SEE
THE SOUNDS OF FINGERBOARD SHAPES—THE EYES HEAR
quotes.why_load_bearing: States Van Eps's chiastic doctrine that physical shape and sound are mutually predictive—the cognitive foundation of his fingerboard-visualization method.

quotes.verbatim_quote: For this concept the fingerboard must be visualized from above.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the specific mental viewpoint required for the shape-transfer studies, distinguishing Van Eps's geometric approach from standard player-perspective notation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A and B are same shape but different notation - B and C are same notation but different shape - C and
D are same shape but different notation etc. It is a form of mutation
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the central phenomenon—'mutation'—that arises when voicings shift between string sets, which the entire string-set chart system is designed to track.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This principle applies to any notation or Voicing that involves the B and G string because of the Major
third in the tuning - If the tuning was all fourths, this difference would not exist - It is this difference
that allows the great variety of notational and fingering choices - what is imposible in one location is

possible in another
quotes.why_load_bearing: Traces the existence of Van Eps's broken/divided string-set vocabulary to a single physical cause—the major third between B and G—which justifies the whole transfer-across-sets methodology.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
ALL THROUGH MY BOOKS THE EMPHASIS WILL BE ON “CHROMATICS”—
CHROMATICS, CHROMATICS, AND MORE CHROMATICS——
quotes.why_load_bearing: Van Eps's own declaration that chromatic inner motion is the defining preoccupation of the entire Harmonic Mechanisms series.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A vertical or angled line thus— / —means “*Set"’—
The number in front of it is the set number—

The number after it indicates the number of strings employed—
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the notational grammar (e.g., 1/3, 2/B3) that every fingering callout in the book uses to specify which strings carry a voicing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the Ist-2nd-4th-strings are employed (skipping the 3rd string) it is termed a broken set —the symbol is:

1/B3—The first set of broken three—etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the 'broken' set concept that lets Van Eps notate non-adjacent three-string voicings, a prerequisite for his open-voicing triad work in later chapters.
